content.text: Neural networks cannot operate on raw text, categories, or identifiers — they require

. An embedding converts discrete objects such as words, tokens, sentences, users, or products into dense floating-point vectors in a high-dimensional space where geometric proximity encodes semantic similarity. Every large language model and every modern agent pipeline depends on embeddings at multiple layers: the input token lookup, contextual hidden states produced by transformer blocks, and the sentence-level vectors used for retrieval, routing, and memory.
An embedding is a learned mapping from a discrete input (a token ID, a document, an entity) into a continuous real-valued vector

is the embedding dimension, typically 256–4096. The mapping is learned rather than hand-crafted: during training, the embedding vectors are adjusted by gradient descent so that semantically related items land near each other according to some distance metric, most commonly cosine similarity.
There are two distinct embedding types relevant to agents and LLMs.
are the first layer of every transformer model — a lookup table of shape

is vocabulary size (e.g. 50,257 for GPT-2; 128,256 for Llama 3). Each token ID indexes a row to produce the vector fed to the first transformer block. These embeddings are updated during model pretraining and are never exposed as standalone outputs.

, produced by dedicated encoder models (e.g. text-embedding-3-small, all-MiniLM-L6-v2), compress an entire sequence into a single fixed-size vector by pooling hidden states. These are the embeddings used in retrieval, vector databases, and agent memory systems.

Embeddings also extend beyond text. Image encoders (CLIP, SigLIP) produce visual embeddings that inhabit the same vector space as text, enabling cross-modal similarity. Code embeddings, trained on repository data, can retrieve semantically similar functions by description. The architectural principle is identical across modalities: learn a dense vector representation such that related items cluster in the resulting space.

Agents must constantly compare, retrieve, and route information by meaning rather than by exact string match. A user asking 'how do I rotate my API key?' and a help article titled 'Regenerating API credentials' share no words in common yet are semantically identical. Without embeddings, finding the relevant article requires either a brittle keyword index or brute-force LLM calls over every document — neither is practical at scale.

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), the dominant pattern for grounding agents in proprietary knowledge, is entirely built on embeddings. At index time, documents are embedded and stored in a vector database. At query time, the user message is embedded and compared against the index to retrieve the most semantically relevant chunks, which are then injected into the prompt context. The quality of retrieval — and therefore the accuracy of the agent's answer — depends directly on the quality of the embedding model relative to the domain.

Beyond RAG, embeddings power several other agent mechanisms: router agents embed user intents and match them to tool descriptions; memory systems embed past interactions to surface relevant history; deduplication pipelines embed outputs to detect near-identical responses. Understanding how embeddings work is a prerequisite for diagnosing and improving any of these systems.
— items close in embedding space are similar in meaning. This enables fuzzy, meaning-based search without domain-specific rules.
— a sentence-level embedding model produces a vector of constant dimension regardless of the input length, enabling uniform storage and indexing.
— embedding parameters are differentiable, so they can be jointly optimized with downstream task objectives via backpropagation.
— pretrained embeddings from large-scale corpora capture general language structure that transfers to specialized tasks with little or no fine-tuning.
— approximate nearest-neighbor algorithms (FAISS, ScaNN, HNSW) can search billions of vectors in milliseconds using embedding representations.
— multimodal models learn embeddings where image and text regions that describe the same concept are geometrically adjacent, enabling image-to-text and text-to-image retrieval.
bytes. One million documents at 1536 dimensions uses 6 GB of RAM — index sharding or quantization is then necessary.

— an embedding model trained on general web text will underperform on specialized domains such as radiology reports, legal contracts, or code. Retrieval quality can degrade severely when the query distribution drifts from training data.
— high cosine similarity measures surface linguistic overlap, not logical equivalence or factual truth. Two contradictory sentences can score 0.9+ if they use the same vocabulary.

— once a document is embedded, the vector does not update when the document changes. Re-indexing is necessary after any update, and embedding model upgrades require full re-embedding of the entire corpus.
— the embedding of 'not helpful' can be close to 'helpful' rather than far from it, depending on training. Negation and logical operators are weakly captured.
Token embeddings are implemented as a matrix multiplication in disguise. The embedding matrix
stores one row per vocabulary token. For a token with ID
is a one-hot vector, but the lookup implementation avoids materializing the one-hot vector. During pretraining, the gradient signal flows back through the transformer layers into
, updating the embedding for each token seen in the training batch.
Sentence embeddings are produced by running a full transformer encoder over the token sequence and then pooling the resulting hidden states. The two dominant pooling strategies are: (1)
— use the hidden state of the special [CLS] token, which transformers like BERT train to aggregate sequence-level information; and (2)
— average all token hidden states, optionally weighted by an attention mask to ignore padding. Mean pooling generally outperforms CLS pooling for asymmetric tasks such as query-to-document matching.
, which measures the angle between two vectors and is invariant to their magnitudes:
When both vectors are L2-normalized (unit norm), cosine similarity reduces to a plain dot product:
. This is important in practice — normalizing once at embedding time means similarity can be computed with a single matrix multiplication, which is extremely fast on GPU or vectorized CPU.
Contrastive training and why embeddings cluster semantically

Modern sentence embedding models are trained with a contrastive objective: given a query and a relevant document (positive pair), pull their embeddings together; given a query and an irrelevant document (negative pair), push them apart. The dominant loss is
(also called NT-Xent or multi-negative ranking loss):

is a temperature hyperparameter (typically 0.01–0.05 for embedding models) controlling how sharply the distribution concentrates around the most similar document. Lower temperature produces more confident rankings but can cause training instability if negatives are too easy. Large embedding models (e.g. text-embedding-3-large) are trained on hundreds of billions of token pairs with hard-negative mining, where the hardest negatives are the documents most confusingly similar to the query.

attrs.alt: Embedding pipeline: from input text through tokenizer, token IDs, embedding matrix, dense token vectors, mean pooling to a sentence vector, then cosine similarity ranking against an indexed corpus.

content.text: The diagram above shows two complementary views of the embedding pipeline. The top row follows a single input query from raw text through tokenization to token IDs, then through the embedding matrix lookup to produce a sequence of token vectors. A pooling step (mean or CLS) collapses the sequence into a fixed-size sentence vector. The bottom-left panel illustrates what the resulting vector space looks like: semantically related queries cluster together, while unrelated topics occupy different regions. The bottom-right panel shows the practical outcome — the query vector is compared against all indexed document vectors, and documents are ranked by cosine similarity score.
Two separate embedding model deployments are often needed: one running at
(batched, offline, high throughput) to embed the knowledge corpus, and one running at
(online, low latency, single inputs). Both must use the identical model and preprocessing pipeline — any mismatch corrupts similarity scores.

Dedicated sentence/document embeddings are the right choice whenever the task requires comparing, retrieving, or clustering items by meaning at a scale that makes LLM calls impractical. Concrete scenarios: building a knowledge base for RAG (embed all documents once, query repeatedly at low cost); semantic search over a product catalog or support ticket archive; clustering user queries to discover intent patterns; deduplicating an agent's memory store; ranking tool descriptions by relevance to the current task context.

Use embeddings when: the corpus is large enough that keyword search produces too many false positives; the query vocabulary differs systematically from the document vocabulary (paraphrase mismatch); or multiple retrieval calls happen per agent turn and per-call LLM inference would exceed latency or cost budgets.
When not to rely on embeddings alone

Embeddings are a poor fit for tasks that require exact matching (identifiers, UUIDs, version numbers), structured filtering (SQL-style WHERE clauses on metadata), or strict logical reasoning over retrieved facts. Hybrid retrieval systems combine dense embedding search with sparse BM25 or keyword search, then rerank the merged results — this typically outperforms either approach in isolation. Do not use embeddings to represent negation-sensitive queries ('items NOT discontinued') without explicit mitigation, as negation is poorly captured by cosine similarity.

A minimal production-ready embedding pipeline has four components: a consistent embedding model, a batched encoding step, a vector store, and a query-time retrieval function. The steps below build this from scratch using the OpenAI embeddings API and an in-memory NumPy index, then extend to FAISS for larger corpora.
Choose an embedding model appropriate for the domain and latency budget.
Preprocess documents consistently: strip HTML, normalize whitespace, and apply any domain-specific cleaning.
Chunk documents to fit within the model's token limit (512–8192 tokens depending on model). Overlap chunks by 10–20% to avoid splitting context across boundaries.
Embed documents in batches (typically 32–512 at a time) to maximize throughput.
Store vectors alongside metadata in a vector database (FAISS, Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector).
At query time, embed the query with the same model and retrieve top-K by cosine similarity.
Post-filter or rerank results using metadata constraints or a cross-encoder reranker.

import numpy as np
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def embed_texts(texts: list[str], model: str = "text-embedding-3-small") -> np.ndarray:
    """Embed a list of texts, returning shape (N, d) float32 array."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(model=model, input=texts)
    vecs = [item.embedding for item in sorted(response.data, key=lambda x: x.index)]
    return np.array(vecs, dtype=np.float32)

def build_index(documents: list[str]) -> tuple[np.ndarray, list[str]]:
    """Embed documents and L2-normalize for cosine similarity via dot product."""
    vecs = embed_texts(documents)
    norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    return vecs / norms, documents

def retrieve(query: str, index: np.ndarray, docs: list[str], top_k: int = 5):
    """Retrieve top-k documents by cosine similarity to query."""
    q_vec = embed_texts([query])
    q_norm = q_vec / np.linalg.norm(q_vec)
    scores = index @ q_norm.T  # (N,)
    top_indices = np.argsort(scores.ravel())[::-1][:top_k]
    return [(docs[i], float(scores[i])) for i in top_indices]

# Usage
docs = [
    "To rotate API credentials, open Settings > API Keys and click Regenerate.",
    "Our refund policy allows returns within 30 days of purchase.",
    "API keys can be scoped to specific endpoints for security.",
]
index, stored_docs = build_index(docs)
results = retrieve("How do I reset my API key?", index, stored_docs)
for doc, score in results:
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {doc}")
Example 1 — Cosine similarity with sentence-transformers (local model)

The sentence-transformers library provides a unified interface to hundreds of pretrained encoder models. This example computes pairwise similarity between sentences to illustrate how semantically related sentences cluster in embedding space.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 80 MB, runs on CPU

sentences = [
    "How do I cancel my subscription?",       # auth/billing query
    "I want to stop paying monthly.",          # semantically same
    "What is the refund policy?",              # billing, adjacent
    "How do I train a neural network?",        # unrelated
    "Cancel my account and stop charges.",     # paraphrase of [0]
]

# encode returns shape (N, 384), normalized=True applies L2 normalisation
vecs = model.encode(sentences, normalize_embeddings=True)

# Cosine similarity matrix (dot product on normalized vectors)
sim_matrix = vecs @ vecs.T

print("Similarity matrix (row i, col j = sim(sentence_i, sentence_j)):")
for i, sent in enumerate(sentences):
    row = "  ".join(f"{sim_matrix[i, j]:.2f}" for j in range(len(sentences)))
    print(f"  [{i}] {row}")

# Most similar pair to sentence[0]
i = 0
sims = [(j, sim_matrix[i, j]) for j in range(len(sentences)) if j != i]
sims.sort(key=lambda x: -x[1])
print(f"\nMost similar to '{sentences[i]}':")
for j, score in sims[:3]:
    print(f"  {score:.3f}  '{sentences[j]}'")
Example 2 — RAG retrieval index with FAISS

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) builds an approximate nearest-neighbor index over embedding vectors for efficient large-scale retrieval. This example demonstrates indexing a corpus and performing low-latency top-K search.

import faiss
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
dim = 384  # embedding dimension for this model

# ── Index construction ──
corpus = [
    "Python list comprehensions create lists from iterables concisely.",
    "NumPy provides N-dimensional array operations for numerical computing.",
    "FAISS enables fast approximate nearest-neighbor search over vectors.",
    "Gradient descent minimizes a loss function by following its negative gradient.",
    "Transformers use self-attention to model token interdependencies.",
    "The Adam optimizer adapts learning rates per parameter using momentum.",
    "Cosine similarity measures angle-based closeness between vectors.",
    "Fine-tuning adjusts pretrained weights for a specific downstream task.",
]

corpus_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype("float32")

# IndexFlatIP: exact inner product (= cosine on normalized vectors)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index.add(corpus_vecs)
print(f"Index contains {index.ntotal} vectors")

# ── Query ──
queries = [
    "How does the Adam optimizer work?",
    "What is a transformer attention mechanism?",
]

for query in queries:
    q_vec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype("float32")
    scores, indices = index.search(q_vec, k=3)
    print(f"\nQuery: {query!r}")
    for rank, (idx, score) in enumerate(zip(indices[0], scores[0]), 1):
        print(f"  {rank}. ({score:.3f}) {corpus[idx]}")

A developer-tools company builds an agent that answers questions over a codebase containing 25,000 Python files with inline docstrings and a 3,000-page architecture wiki. A purely keyword-based search fails because developers ask conceptual questions ('where is the retry logic?') that match no exact identifiers. The engineering team implements a two-stage RAG pipeline:

Stage 1 (offline): each file is split into function-level chunks of at most 512 tokens with 64-token overlap. All 180,000 chunks are embedded using text-embedding-3-large (3072 dimensions), compressed to 1536 dimensions via matryoshka projection, and stored in a Qdrant vector database with metadata (file path, git SHA, function name). The indexing job runs 45 minutes on a single A10 GPU using batch size 256.

Stage 2 (online): at query time, the user's natural-language question is embedded (mean pooling, same model), and the top-20 chunks are retrieved by cosine similarity. A cross-encoder reranker (ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2) rescores the 20 candidates and selects the top-5, which are injected into the prompt context. Median end-to-end latency is 280 ms: 40 ms for embedding, 18 ms for vector search, 210 ms for reranking, and 10 ms overhead.

from qdrant_client import QdrantClient, models
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, CrossEncoder

EMBED_MODEL = "text-embedding-3-large"  # via OpenAI client
RERANK_MODEL = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")
COLLECTION = "codebase_chunks"

client = QdrantClient(url="http://localhost:6333")

def retrieve_and_rerank(query: str, top_dense: int = 20, top_k: int = 5):
    from openai import OpenAI
    oai = OpenAI()

    # Stage 1: dense retrieval
    q_vec = oai.embeddings.create(
        model=EMBED_MODEL, input=query
    ).data[0].embedding

    hits = client.search(
        collection_name=COLLECTION,
        query_vector=q_vec,
        limit=top_dense,
        with_payload=True,
    )
    candidates = [(h.payload["text"], h.payload["file_path"]) for h in hits]

    # Stage 2: cross-encoder reranking
    pairs = [(query, text) for text, _ in candidates]
    rerank_scores = RERANK_MODEL.predict(pairs)
    ranked = sorted(
        zip(rerank_scores, candidates), key=lambda x: -x[0]
    )[:top_k]

    return [
        {"score": float(score), "text": text, "file": path}
        for score, (text, path) in ranked
    ]

results = retrieve_and_rerank("Where is the retry logic for HTTP requests?")
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['score']:.3f}  {r['file']}  {r['text'][:80]}...")
Mistake 1 — Skipping normalization before dot-product search

Cosine similarity and raw dot product give the same ranking only when all vectors have unit norm. Using unnormalized vectors with a dot-product index (as FAISS IndexFlatIP does) favors high-magnitude vectors, producing incorrect rankings. Always normalize before indexing and before querying.

import numpy as np

# Wrong: dot product without normalization
a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])   # norm = 5.0
b = np.array([0.6, 0.8])   # norm = 1.0
c = np.array([0.5, 0.0])   # norm = 0.5

# a and b point in the same direction (cosine sim = 1.0)
# but a @ b = 5.0, a @ c = 1.5, b @ c = 0.3
print("Dot products:", a @ b, a @ c, b @ c)          # 5.0, 1.5, 0.3

# Correct: normalize first
def normalize(v): return v / np.linalg.norm(v)
a_n, b_n, c_n = normalize(a), normalize(b), normalize(c)
print("Cosine sims:", a_n @ b_n, a_n @ c_n, b_n @ c_n)  # 1.0, 0.6, 0.6
# Now a and b are correctly identified as identical in direction
Mistake 2 — Using different models at index and query time
Each embedding model maps text into a unique coordinate system. Mixing models — for example, indexing with
— produces vectors in incompatible spaces. Similarity scores become meaningless. This also applies to model version upgrades: re-embed the entire corpus when switching models.
Mistake 3 — Embedding documents that exceed the model's context window

Most embedding models silently truncate inputs longer than their maximum token limit (commonly 512 or 8192 tokens). A 20,000-token legal contract embedded as a single input will produce a vector representing only the first 512 tokens. The solution is to chunk the document before embedding and aggregate retrieved chunks at query time, not to embed the full document and hope for the best.
Mistake 4 — Treating cosine similarity as factual confidence

A retrieved document with cosine similarity 0.92 is semantically similar to the query — it does not mean the document correctly answers the query or is factually accurate. Embedding models do not distinguish truth from plausible-sounding falsehood. Always route retrieved content through an LLM for generation and apply grounding checks.

before choosing a model. Run the MTEB benchmark subsets (BEIR for retrieval, STSb for similarity) on examples from the target domain. General leaderboard scores do not predict domain-specific performance.
, not at fixed boundaries. Overlap of 10–20% of chunk size prevents critical context from being split across chunk boundaries.

when the model requires it. Instructed embedding models (e2e-instruct, GTE-Qwen) expect a task prefix on the query side (e.g. 'Represent this question for searching answers: ...') but not on the document side. Check the model card.
. Document embeddings are deterministic: cache them by a hash of (model, preprocessing version, text). Re-embed only on model or preprocessing changes.
Use matryoshka embeddings to trade accuracy for size

. Models trained with Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) allow truncating embedding dimensions at inference time — truncating text-embedding-3-large from 3072 to 256 dimensions loses only ~4% retrieval accuracy while cutting storage and search cost by 12×.
Apply metadata filtering before or after dense retrieval
to enforce hard constraints (date ranges, category labels, access permissions) that embedding similarity cannot express.
periodically by running a set of known-good query-document pairs and checking that the correct documents appear in the top-5. Retrieval accuracy should be part of the production monitoring dashboard.

The cost of embedding-based retrieval has two components: offline indexing and online query serving. Indexing throughput with a GPU embedding server typically reaches 5,000–50,000 tokens per second depending on model size. A 100 million-token corpus can be indexed in under an hour on a single A10 GPU. Query-time embedding adds 5–50 ms of latency for a typical sentence.
The critical bottleneck at scale is nearest-neighbor search over large indexes. Exact search via

per query — 10 million vectors at 1536 dimensions requires ~60 billion floating-point operations per query, which takes several seconds on CPU. Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms reduce this dramatically.
The three dominant ANN approaches are:
— graph-based index, O(log N) query time, high recall, high memory usage (~64 bytes per vector + graph overhead). Used by Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus.
— clusters vectors into Voronoi cells during indexing, searches only the k nearest cells at query time. FAISS IndexIVFFlat achieves 10–100× speedup with 5–15% recall loss.
— compresses each vector into a compact code by quantizing subvectors independently. Reduces memory by 8–32× at the cost of 5–20% recall. Combined with IVF as IndexIVFPQ for large-scale deployments.

import faiss
import numpy as np

dim = 1536
n_vectors = 1_000_000

# Simulate corpus (in production, these are real embeddings)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
corpus_vecs = rng.random((n_vectors, dim), dtype="float32")
# Normalize for cosine similarity
faiss.normalize_L2(corpus_vecs)

# IVF + PQ index: ~100x faster than exact search, ~16x smaller
n_centroids = 4096          # typically sqrt(N)
n_subquantizers = 64        # each 24-float subvector → 1 byte
bits_per_code = 8

quantizer = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantizer, dim, n_centroids, n_subquantizers, bits_per_code)
index.train(corpus_vecs)    # requires ~10M training vectors
index.add(corpus_vecs)

# At query time — set nprobe to control recall/speed trade-off
index.nprobe = 64           # search 64 of 4096 clusters; higher = more accurate
query = rng.random((1, dim), dtype="float32")
faiss.normalize_L2(query)
scores, indices = index.search(query, k=10)
print(f"Top result index: {indices[0, 0]}, score: {scores[0, 0]:.4f}")

Memory and throughput estimates for 10 million 1536-dimensional vectors at float32: exact storage is 60 GB, HNSW adds ~8 GB of graph, IVF+PQ(64,8) compresses to ~620 MB with ~94% recall@10. For most production RAG workloads with corpora under 1 million documents, exact search is fast enough.

The embedding ecosystem has standardized around a small set of well-maintained libraries and platforms. Choosing the right tool depends on whether the embedding model runs locally or via API, and whether the vector store needs to be embedded in process or operate as a separate service.

— the de-facto standard for local open-weight embedding models. Provides hundreds of pretrained models, handles tokenization and pooling, and integrates with HuggingFace Hub. Best for on-premises deployments or when API cost is a concern.
(text-embedding-3-small / large) — highest-quality general-purpose embeddings as a managed API. Supports Matryoshka dimension reduction. Simple to integrate; incurs per-token cost.
— strong multilingual performance; supports 100+ languages with a single model. Useful for global deployments.
— Meta's C++ library with Python bindings for high-performance approximate nearest-neighbor search. Supports CPU and GPU, several index types, and billion-scale corpora.
— vector database with filtering, payload support, sparse-dense hybrid search, and a REST/gRPC API. Runs as a Docker container or managed cloud service.
— PostgreSQL extension adding a vector type and IVFFlat/HNSW indexes. Ideal for teams that want to keep vectors in an existing Postgres database without a separate service.
— RAG orchestration frameworks with adapters for many embedding models and vector stores, enabling pipeline construction without boilerplate.
Embedding systems introduce several failure modes that differ from conventional software failures. Understanding and mitigating them is essential for production agent deployments.

— research has demonstrated that it is possible to approximately reconstruct the original text from its embedding vector using a trained decoder model (vec2text). This is a concrete privacy risk: avoid storing raw embeddings of sensitive user data (PII, health records) in externally accessible indexes. Apply access controls at the vector database level, not just at the API gateway.

— an adversary who can insert documents into the retrieval corpus can craft inputs that, when embedded, cause their malicious content to rank first for high-value queries (embedding-space adversarial examples). Mitigate by validating and sanitizing all documents before indexing, and by restricting write access to the corpus.

— when the embedding model is updated, previously computed vectors become incompatible with new query vectors. Vectors from different model versions must never coexist in the same index. Maintain a version label on every index, automate full re-indexing on model change, and use blue-green deployment to switch indexes atomically.

— unlike database errors, poor retrieval quality does not throw exceptions. The LLM may generate confidently wrong answers based on irrelevant retrieved context. Instrument retrieval with: (1) similarity score thresholds — reject retrievals below a minimum score rather than passing low-quality context; (2) periodic retrieval accuracy monitoring using held-out labeled pairs; (3) human-in-the-loop spot checks for high-stakes deployments.

— each document chunk and each query call to an embedding API incurs cost. Implement request batching, embedding caches keyed by content hash, and per-tenant rate limits to prevent runaway API spend or inadvertent denial of service.
that map discrete objects into a continuous space where geometry encodes semantics — items near each other in the space are similar in meaning.
Token embeddings and sentence embeddings serve different roles
: token embeddings are internal to the LLM's forward pass; sentence embeddings are produced by dedicated encoder models and used for retrieval, clustering, and memory.
is the standard metric. Normalize once at embedding time so similarity reduces to a fast dot product at query time.
on large paired datasets using InfoNCE loss, which pulls positive pairs together and pushes negatives apart. Quality depends heavily on training data domain coverage.
Approximate nearest-neighbor indexes (FAISS, HNSW) scale retrieval
to millions of documents with sub-millisecond query latency. For corpora under ~500K documents, exact search is fast enough.
Always use the same model and preprocessing for indexing and querying
. Mixing models or versions produces incompatible coordinate systems and meaningless similarity scores.
Hybrid retrieval (dense + BM25) outperforms either approach alone
on most production benchmarks. Use embeddings for semantic matching and BM25 for exact-term coverage, then rerank the merged candidates.

: guard against index poisoning, apply similarity score thresholds to avoid returning irrelevant context, monitor retrieval accuracy continuously, and treat embedding drift after model upgrades as an operational incident.
